sentences.text: He was a classmate of mine, 17, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a kid who could see the funny side of everything, always laughed.
It was just bad luck.
It wasn't a round-up.
He was going home one night and the Arrow Cross [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] wanted papers, asking for papers.
He pulled a gun instead.
He had a gun on him.
And they threw a hand grenade at him and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] completely obliterated him.
There wasn't enough left to bury.
I can't remember.
I can't remember who told me.
He lived very close to us.
And his blood was on the side of that building for the next 25 years, no kidding.
No.
No.
Yes.
Ishveld, Yuri.
I think it must have been in January, close to liberation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December of '44 or January '45.
Not really, no.
We were good mates.
He was good company.
Yeah.
Well, the war-- the end of the war was nearing for us, not the end of the war-- I mean, or, OK, in January '45, obviously it wasn't too far away.
But for us, once the Russians arrived, that was the end of it.
Well, look, there was constant fireworks.
And for six months, we went to sleep with the sound of gunfire, cannons, you name it, aerial bombardment.
And you got so used to it that you woke up if-- it it stopped.
And the streets were dangerous because of the Arrow Cross.
They concentrated on Jews always.
But towards the end, they were concentrating on escaped [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] deserters from the army.
Of course, people weren't stupid.
They weren't going to get killed for nothing.
They changed into civilian clothes and tried to get away.
If the Arrow Cross caught them, they hung them on the nearest tree or lamp post.
The trees on the Boulevard were festooned with-- with deserters [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and-- and usually with a placard on their chest, that's what happens to deserters.
And in general, the situation was-- it was a shambles.
No windows in-- no glass in the windows anywhere.
They were all blown out ages ago.
No water, no food, no electricity, no nothing.
I don't know.
I honestly don't know.
With great difficulty.
Look, now that you ask me, I remember my mother found a bit of flour and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow, we managed to get a-- a wood-fired stove, I wouldn't have a clue from where.
And we stuck the flue out the non-existent window.
And the house opposite was bombed to the ground.
It was a five-story house, but a bomb demolished it.
And we got the parquetry floor from there and heated the-- the-- fueled the stove with that.
And then my mother mixed up the flour with water and cooked that on-- or fried it or whatever on-- on the top of it without anything.
And that was OK as long as it lasted.
No.
By-- towards the end, food was very scarce.
On January the 18th, 1945.
Well, look, we didn't know.
And we heard Russian spoken in the night.
And we didn't know whether it was Russian prisoners of war or Russian soldiers.
That night I was at home, in our house.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really close Russian soldiers, all right.
In daylight, we could see that there was Russians everywhere.
And German soldiers were walking without any escort to the prisoner of war camps.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the Jews in our neighborhood, that was-- that really was a holiday.
And the first Russians who arrived there were really very decent.
They had a field kitchen with them.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they cooked a Hungarian goulash.
And the ritual was that any Jew who went down with a yellow star, the Russian in charge got his yellow star off and gave him a-- a plate full of goulash.
And of course it happened that a Jew put another coat on with a yellow star and he went down for a second.
If they recognized it, he got a belting instead of a plate of soup.
They were OK.
They were really decent.
But then after three days or so, the occupation troops arrived who were absolutely dreadful.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They robbed and pillaged and raped and whatnot.
And if they could beat a Jew, they do it.
They wouldn't kill you, but they give you a beating.
Oh, much better.
Your life wasn't in danger, unless-- it has happened that people were killed by drunken Russians.
No.
No.
Hungarians attacking Germans, no.
Retribution against Germans?
No.
No.
Well, if the Russians caught SS, they routinely killed them on the spot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers went into prisoner of war camps.
The Wehrmacht, yeah.
And the chances are most of them never came back.
They took them to camps in Russia.
They were badly treated.
A lot of them never came back.
The trouble was that the Russians were not particularly discerning.
They didn't ask for your IDs, ID papers, whether you were a soldier or not.
Jew or Gentile, if you were in the age group, they carted you off to a prisoner of war camp.
Many Jews were caught by the Russians, Jews who just managed to barely by the skin of their teeth to arrive, and finished up in a Russian camp in-- in Russia.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
We had no idea.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had no idea.
And at that time, all we knew about Auschwitz was what we read on that leaflet from President Roosevelt.
And I remember musing about it and talking to a friend, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was probably taken to Gestapo headquarters in Buda.
From there, they probably took him to Auschwitz.
It would have taken so and so long to get there.
We knew that Auschwitz was liberated the same time-- the same day as us, January the 18th.
And I thought the old man was a resourceful fellow.
He may have managed to survive in Auschwitz.
And if so, he should be back in Budapest any day now.
And without me knowing, he was already back in Budapest then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was liberated in Auschwitz.
He managed to hide under one of the-- of the barracks.
And as soon as the Russians arrived, he started the walk home.
It took him a month.
He was blood and skin and bone.
And-- but he got back.
My brother was taken prisoner in Russia.
There was a big breakthrough and the Russians took the whole of the Hungarian Army, the whole Army group as prisoners.
And eventually in the end, they sorted out Jews from non-Jews.
But at the beginning, they had nowhere to keep their prisoners.
Most of them froze to death.
The ones who didn't freeze to death were-- died of typhus in the spring.
And I was told that in the division that my brother was in, there were 15,000 Jews and 3 came back.
He came back on the Day of Atonement, on Kol Nidre night in 1945.
Well, that would have been almost a month, in February then, towards the end of February in '44.
And he was the first person that I heard from gas chambers and whatever else in-- in Auschwitz.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
I had to believe it then.
No.
We didn't know until he actually arrived.
Look, I think my parents would have decided to stay there.
Whether they decided at that stage-- there wasn't a lot of choice, you know?
I mean, where do you go, and particularly for middle-aged people.
I decided very early in the peace that I had enough.
I wouldn't stay there.
And I can even tell you the time when it happened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was already going to school then.
The school reopened very soon after the Russians arrived.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were hardly any kids, hardly any teachers.
But the school reopened.
And I was going to school.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a policeman pulled me up.
He was a policeman who was taking prisoners for the Russians.
And it so happened I recognized this policeman as one of the policemen who was escorting Jews to the ghetto.
No.
No.
A policeman in a police uniform.
And you know, when the Russians arrived, I watched the police march past the Russians.
The Russians marched them out.
The Russians took their rifles and they bashed them against the pavement, making them useless, so they were not armed.
And I recognized this cop.
And I called him-- I lost my temper and I called him a no-good son of a bitch.
You were working for the-- for the Germans and now you're working for the Russians.
I said, I recognize you.
You were escorting Jews not that long ago.
And then he lost his temper.
And he said, on your way, before I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do something.
And that was when I made up my time, this is it.
This-- this is no place for me.
Oh look, years-- you know, if you survived that one year under German occupation, you were an old man, believe me.
You accumulated enough experiences to last-- last your lifetime.
In fact, school was impossible, you know?
It was impossible to keep discipline with kids who survived the occupation.
Yes, I did.
I decided nothing.
But I-- the only thing that I decided was not to stay there any longer, no matter what.
As a first stage, I thought I'd go to Vienna.
I didn't even apply to the university in-- in Budapest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided to get out and try and study in Vienna.
Of course, in retrospect, it was damn stupid.
Were the Austrians any better than the Hungarians?
No.
I know that now.
If anything, perhaps they were worse.
Well, we were all dressed up, as I said, minutes away from starting to walk to the synagogue.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a knock on the door.
And there was a-- a Russian soldier standing outside.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't recognize my brother at all.
It was my brother, in a Russian uniform.
And my first thought was, another bloody Russian came robbing the house, because that was an everyday occurrence.
And then, of course, he must have introduced himself.
Nearing on four years, I would say.
We didn't even know he was alive in that-- in that time.
I honestly can't remember if we went to shul or whether we stayed home.
I'd have to ask my brother.
He would probably remember?
It's difficult to put it into words.
As I mentioned to you before, I have never, ever met a Jewish family where every member survived under German occupation.
Ours is the only one.
And it must have been quite something to see the whole family complete.
Yeah.
Oh look, that really had nothing to do with it.
I imagine if you were a believer, you probably stayed a believer.
But the trouble was that in a Jewish school, they pushed Judaism down our throat to such an extent that they managed to turn most of us against it.
Attendance at the synagogue was compulsory, every Shabbos, every Friday night.
And there was a roll call.
You know, they really put us off.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that had more to do with it than-- than the German occupation.
Well, I do remember when my father came back from Auschwitz-- I remember him saying there can't be a God, or he couldn't have permitted this to happen.
And yet the he went back to being a believer again.
My mother was always a believer.
After matriculation.
As soon as I finished school, I got out like a shot.
No.
Must have been-- look, the school year started in Vienna in September.
I must have got out of Budapest in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I expect July or August.
But I don't honestly remember exactly.
How do you mean?
No.
I went to Australia illegally.
There was no legal way.
There was nobody to give you a passport, and nothing like that.
And I went to Vienna illegally.
How did I get there?
I can't remember how I got to the border.
I just walked across the border.
There was only Russians to worry about borders then, and they didn't particularly worry.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got a local train to Vienna.
And I stayed at first with friends of my father's who knew them from way back.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was in the American zone and I got papers after a while.
Later on I managed to get myself a Hungarian passport, so for a while I could go back in school holidays.
No.
I perceived less anti-Semitism in Vienna than in Budapest, but I was probably wrong.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see what you want to see.
And I was also more familiar with Budapest, so I saw more of it.
And certainly at the University, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if-- for instance, the professors were all old men, either men who retired before the Nazis took over or Jews who came back from overseas.
And the students were all veterans, most of them older than I.
They were usually men in their-- from 25 to 30 who really wanted to get on with their lives.
They were probably in a prison camp or in the army or whatever.
And most of them were years older than I.
And Vienna itself was bombed to ribbons.
It wasn't much to look at in those days.
Food was very scarce.
There was starvation for years after.
I can't think of any in-- in Vienna.
I do remember in Hungary, even when I traveled on train, listening to people talk-- more than once I heard more came back then the Germans ever deported.
That-- that was common sentiment.
No.
At first I stayed in a place in Bankgasse and then in Neubaugasse.
It so happens that was the same street a block further on from where my uncle had his factory before he was sent to Dachau.
I got a scholarship from-- if I remember rightly, if it was from HIAS, but I can't be too sure.
It was a Jewish organization, at any rate.
It would have been Austrian Jewish organization that would have been financed, I imagine, internationally, because Austrian Jews then had nothing.
Civil engineering.
No problems at all, none at all.
Well, look, the system in Vienna was different from here.
The entrance examination consisted of the dean having a talk with you for a half an hour or so.
If he liked your face, you were admitted.
None.
None.
Some of the lecturers were in the Army.
As I said, the professors were old.
The lecturers were, as usual, in their 30s, 40s, all veterans.
And my mathematics lecturer I knew well.
He was in the-- what do you call it?
The code department, you know, trying to break Allied codes in the Air Force.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
There were a couple of blokes I knew, colleagues, who were-- I knew were SS, who didn't know I was Jewish and told me.
I didn't advertise my Jewishness.
I don't think that would have been advisable.
They told me they were in the SS.
But they were Volksdeutsche from Slovakia.
The ethnic Germans were routinely drafted into the Waffen-SS.
Pardon me.
[DRINKS] Look, from memory, they neither-- they were neither proud or upset about it.
For them, it was just a fact of life.
One of them was in a bad way, come to think of it now.
He had diabetes very badly.
He said he was in a tank and the tank was shot up and caught fire.
And he had diabetes ever since.
Look, at that time, that wasn't discussed.
Now--
No, not really.
Not really.
Look, today that-- that would seem quite outlandish.
But you've got to remember that Dr.
Mengele went back to his town and lived under his own name for the next five years and nobody worried about him.
Jews went home.
They knew all about Dr.
Mengele.
And nobody bothered about it.
Look, they were too busy trying to make a living.
And so for-- and-- as well as that somehow the enormity of what happened didn't sink in.
It kept sinking in 10 years after the event, actually what we went through.
Oh, without stopping, all the time.
No, that I wouldn't remember now.
But naturally, experiences were exchanged.
Look, to be a-- a student in Vienna in your late teens or 20s, you had to have a story to had survived.
There was no other way.
You were either in a camp or-- or you were hiding.
There was nothing else.
Yes.
Friends of mine went-- did go to Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My best friend actually, he's a friend to this day, he's in-- in Palestine.
Yes.
He finished medicine in Vienna and went to Palestine.
Joseph [?
Barkoy. ?]
Never.
No.
Look, I-- I fancied myself as a European.
And I didn't like the idea of-- in those days, Zionism had sort of agricultural undertones.
You went to a kibbutz and you the soil.
It wasn't my idea.
There were other things that I objected to, such as the use of Hebrew, Hebrew writing in particular.
To me, that was going back.
I thought that Latin script was efficient enough.
I didn't like this idea.
Ah, very happy that Israel is there.
If I didn't live in Australia, I'd live in Israel.
But I live in Australia and it is my home.
